title-group.article-title: Point‐of‐Care Diagnosis of Cryptosporidiosis in Neonatal Ruminant Faecal Samples by a Portable Field Microscope Integrated With a Smartphone

aff.named-content: Gölhisar Vocational School of Health Services
Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering
Faculty of Engineering and Natural Sciences

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: A portable, lightweight, and compact microscope was developed for rapid on‐site identification of Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts, a major cause of diarrhoea in neonatal ruminants. Cryptosporidiosis is an infectious parasitic disease that causes diarrhoea, and rapid diagnosis plays a critical role in initiating timely treatment. A method for diagnosing cryptosporidiosis involves detecting oocysts excreted in faeces using a light microscope with an appropriate staining method. However, transporting samples to a laboratory often takes time, during which the animal's health may deteriorate. The developed portable microscope provides up to 1000x magnification, enabling the diagnosis of cryptosporidiosis using the carbol fuchsin staining method. It is compatible with almost any smartphone and does not require additional digital or optical magnification. Through an integrated mobile telemedicine application, veterinarians can instantly consult specialists by transmitting patient information and images. Calibration tests have demonstrated that the system can generate high‐resolution images of oocysts. This innovative microscope is a crucial tool for veterinarians working in the field, allowing for rapid detection of Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts, reducing neonatal calf mortality, and preventing the spread of infectious diseases.

A portable, smartphone‐integrated microscope enables rapid, on‐site diagnosis of Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts in neonatal ruminant faeces using carbol fuchsin staining. It provides diagnostic image quality comparable to laboratory microscopes with up to 1000× magnification and ∼1 micrometers (µm) resolution. Integrated mobile software allows real‐time expert consultation, offering a low‐cost, efficient solution for early detection and disease control in remote or large‐scale farming environments.

fn.p: Funding: This work is supported by The Presidency of The Republic of Turkiye Presidency of Strategy and Budget within the framework of “Mission Differentiation and Specialization Program Focused on Regional Development” (Project No: 2017K12‐41003) under the project number 2017K12‐41003‐20.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Neonatal calf diarrhoea remains one of the most critical health issues in young cattle, causing significant nutritional and digestive disturbances that negatively affect growth and productivity. High morbidity and mortality rates associated with the disease lead to considerable economic losses. In untreated cases, mortality can reach up to 50% (Fu et al. 2024). In the authors’ country, approximately 6 million calves are born annually, with neonatal losses reaching 15%, and diarrhoea is responsible for around 56% of these deaths (Ateş and Yeşilbağ 2022). Notably, calf mortality can be as high as 10% in state‐run farms and 50% in private farms (Akyüz et al. 2017), illustrating the widespread and severe impact of the disease on livestock efficiency and production.

Among the infectious agents responsible, Cryptosporidium parvum is widely recognized as a primary cause of neonatal diarrhoea and is also a significant zoonotic pathogen (Qi et al. 2020, Delafosse et al. 2015). Local prevalence ranges between 22.6 and 27.6%, consistent with global reports averaging 21.9% (Chen et al. 2023). Infected calves experience weight loss, growth retardation, and increased veterinary costs in the acute phase, while long‐term effects include decreased productivity, all contributing to substantial economic burdens (Qi et al. 2020, Gunn and Stott 1997, Brainard et al. 2020). Early disease diagnosis is critically important for protecting individual calves' health and preventing transmission at the herd level. Timely interventions reduce treatment costs and minimize production losses. Moreover, the ability of veterinarians to make rapid diagnoses in the field increases the effectiveness of treatment plans, positively impacting both animal welfare and farm economics.

Clinical signs of cryptosporidiosis usually emerge within 3 to 5 days after exposure and may persist up to 17 days. These include diarrhoea, dehydration, fever, and notable imbalances in acid‐base status, alongside changes in haematological and biochemical parameters (Nydam and Mohammed 2005, Klein et al. 2008, Manouana et al. 2020). In endemic areas, when C. parvum is the sole pathogen, morbidity can reach 100%, often resulting in high mortality rates (Klein et al. 2008, Shehata et al. 2024). Age, nutrition, management practices, and concurrent infections influence disease severity.

Despite various available treatments, no completely effective therapeutic agent has been developed. Most drugs tested show limited efficacy, only partially reducing oocyst shedding or clinical symptoms. Adding to the challenge, Cryptosporidium oocysts are highly resistant to environmental stressors and most conventional disinfectants, making disease control difficult (Certad et al. 2024).

The zoonotic nature of Cryptosporidium spp. further emphasizes its public health significance. Infected calves can excrete billions of oocysts into the environment, which are then transmitted through faecal‐oral routes. Human cryptosporidiosis, commonly seen in early childhood and especially prevalent in livestock‐based communities, can originate from such transmission pathways (Nydam et al. 2001, Thomson et al. 2019, Samra et al. 2016). This highlights the importance of rapid and accurate diagnosis for both veterinary and human health.

Diagnosing Cryptosporidium infections may involve light microscopy combined with specific staining protocols. However, access to appropriate laboratory equipment and trained personnel is often lacking in remote or resource‐limited regions, delaying or even preventing diagnosis (Prasad et al. 2021). As a result, there is a clear need for portable, cost‐effective diagnostic technologies that can be deployed in the field.

Recent advances in telemedicine and mobile health technologies have opened new possibilities for point‐of‐care diagnostics. Now ubiquitous among healthcare practitioners, smartphones have become powerful tools for image capture, processing, communication and consultation (Woodward et al. 2002, Braun et al. 2005, Tachakra et al. 2003, Frean 2007, Granot et al. 2008, Jagarapu and Savani 2021). It was previously shown that camera‐enabled mobile phones can capture images through the eyepiece of a standard light microscope (Frean 2007) and that microscopic images can be wirelessly transmitted for further analysis (Zimic et al. 2009). Even before the advent of smartphones, there were already several reports of microscopes attached to mobile phones (Breslauer et al. 2009, Tseng et al. 2010). For instance, a prototype portable fluorescence microscope using a light‐emitting diode (LED) as the light source can capture images obtained from 6 µm fluorescent beads with a condenser lens from a real microscope (Breslauer et al. 2009). In a recent study, a smartphone with a telephoto camera lens for brightfield and fluorescent microscopic imaging was transformed into a portable microscope by attaching an infinity‐corrected objective before the camera lens (Cai et al. 2020). Accordingly, an optical resolution of 4.4 µm was obtained with a 40x objective lens. Zhang et al. (2024) proposed a smartphone microscope for home health monitoring during the COVID‐19 pandemic, utilizing the flexibility of 3D‐printed materials to achieve a focusing accuracy of 5 µm and incorporating an AI–based image enhancement method (Zhang et al. 2024). To enable detailed imaging at the cellular level in clinical laboratory tests and to reduce test result waiting times, a microscope prototype called the μ‐Phone has been introduced. The system provides a depth of field of 5.25 µm, a resolution of 26.3 pixels per µm, and a spatial resolution of 2.19 µm (Song et al. 2024). In addition, Skandarajah et al. (Skandarajah et al. 2014) showed that smartphone microscopes can provide reliable and reproducible images suitable for diagnostic use.

In this context, the present study introduces a portable, compact, and low‐cost light microscope integrated with a smartphone, designed to enable field‐based diagnosis of veterinary infectious diseases such as cryptosporidiosis. Using simple carbol fuchsin staining, the system captures images of Cryptosporidium oocysts in faecal samples and enables remote consultation via a mobile application. This approach supports timely and accurate diagnosis by veterinarians working far from centralized facilities, improving treatment efficiency and reducing mortality through faster clinical response.

sec.title: Sampling Procedure
sec.p: Holstein calves in ………… city with neonatal diarrhoea (3–30 days old) were selected for sampling, either reported by owners or admitted to the local animal hospital. The study took place in early 2023, during high cryptosporidiosis prevalence. Calves were excluded if they: (i) were outside the 3–30 day range, (ii) had chronic diarrhoea or congenital disorders, (iii) showed mixed pathogens in faeces via immunochromatographic kits, (iv) were under antibiotic treatment, or (v) lacked colostrum intake.

Among the remaining, a total of 250 calves for which immunochromatographic test kits indicated cryptosporidiosis alone and Cryptosporidium oocysts were identified under a laboratory microscope (Nikon Eclipse E100) in the animal hospital were selected for further study with the designed portable microscopy system. In other words, the calf sampling was carried out so that all selected calves were diagnosed with cryptosporidiosis in advance through two distinct methods to validate the results obtained through the portable microscope system.

sec.title: Faecal Sample Preparation and Diagnosis
sec.p: Following a systematic clinical examination, faecal samples were non‐invasively collected from diarrheic calves’ rectums using gloves and placed in sterile containers. Personal protective equipment (PPE)—face mask, disposable boot covers, gown, and bonnet—was worn, and hand sanitizer was used before and after the procedure. All PPE and samples were properly disposed of to prevent pathogen spread. The collection followed the calves’ natural defecation process, with owner permission obtained. About 1 mL of faeces was stored per case. Samples were initially inspected visually to confirm diarrhoea.

Cryptosporidiosis was initially diagnosed using immunochromatographic rapid antigen test kits (Bio‐X Diagnostics S.A., Belgium). Two hundred fifty faecal samples testing positive solely for cryptosporidiosis were promptly examined with a laboratory microscope, and then with the designed portable microscope.

The carbol fuchsin staining method for detecting oocysts in stool samples was applied per Heine's protocol (Heine 1982). Oocysts were counted in 20 fields under a light microscope at 1000x magnification, and the mean per field was calculated. Counts were classified as (++++) for >20, (+++) for 6–20, (++) for 1–5, and (+) for <1 oocyst. The stain enhances the visibility of Cryptosporidium spp. by penetrating the thick oocyst wall, rendering them bright against a reddish background, which the stain cannot penetrate. This improves diagnostic accuracy and enables rapid, reliable identification (Weber et al. 1992). The method is easy to perform, cost‐effective, requires no specialized equipment, and is suitable for field use where advanced labs are lacking.

Diagnosis of Cryptosporidium spp. is also possible through standard methods such as Enzyme‐Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA), immunochromatographic tests (IC), polymerase chain reaction (PCR), as well as acid‐fast staining through procedures such as Kinyoun's acid‐fast method or (modified) Ziehl–Neelsen technique (Abdou et al. 2022, Ertaş and Ayan 2021, Yildirim et al. 2021). Among the advantages of the ELISA method are its higher sensitivity and suitability for analysing large sample groups. Its disadvantages include higher cost and potentially lower specificity. In addition, it may also produce false‐positive results. Ability to offer the highest sensitivity and specificity, and its capacity to detect even low amounts of Cryptosporidium spp. agents are among the advantages of the PCR method. However, it is an expensive and complex, requiring laboratory infrastructure and experienced personnel. Another method is the Direct Fluorescent Antibody (DFA) test, which is more sensitive than brightfield microscopy and is widely used, especially in faecal samples. However, it is difficult to perform, requires special microscopes and stains, and can be costly. However, unlike other techniques, the portable microscope in our study provides more practical and faster diagnosis in the field. It can also instantly contact a certified analyst for consultation at the university laboratory and get assistance. In contrast, alternative diagnostic methods (ELISA, IC, PCR, etc.) are unsuitable for on‐site diagnosis. Moreover, the software provides a database for practitioners to keep track of the animals and the occurrence and prevalence of cryptosporidiosis.

The staining solution contains >13.5 g/l fuchsine (C20H20N3·HCl, C.I. 42510), 66.6 g/l phenol (C6H6O), 50 mL/l glycerol (C3H8O3), 50 mL/l dimethyl sulfite (C2H6OS), 166.6 mL/l 96% pure ethanol (C2H5OH), and 150 mL/l isopropyl alcohol (C3H7OH). In the first step, a small stool sample was placed on a microscope slide, and a small amount of the carbol fuchsin solution was dropped onto the sample. The dye and stool sample mixture was then gently mixed with a toothbrush. Afterward, the stained sample was spread onto the slide using another slide. The final sample was left to air dry for 10 min at room temperature. Finally, immersion oil was placed on the dried sample and mounted onto the portable microscope's sample holder. The sample was examined using a 100x objective with the naked eye or through a smartphone.

sec.title: Portable Microscope Design
sec.p: The portable field microscope in operation is shown in Figure 1a. It is an inverted microscope with a specially designed turret mechanism where only the objective in use is exposed to air through a circular hole in the turret casing, Figure 1b. This mechanism helps prevent contamination of the objectives in harsh environments. Three commercially available plan achromatic objectives (Motic Corp.) i.e., a 10x, a 40x, and a 100x (oil), were employed. In addition, a widefield Ramsden type 10x eyepiece was employed. The light path within the portable microscope was designed to isolate it from environmental light sources as much as possible, as seen in Figure 1b. Two planar mirrors in Figure 1b with front surfaces coated with aluminium with less than λ/4 roughness from the Thorlabs Company were employed to obtain high‐resolution images. The present design is an example of a folded‐optics microscope of the McArthur type (Goren 2013, Vasiman et al. 2019, Gordon et al. 2022). The overall size of the bare case is 124 ×78 × 96 mm, where the total size is larger due to adjustment knobs, etc., of the stages in Figure 1a.

caption.p: (a) Photograph of the portable microscope used to identify Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts in faecal samples stained by carbol fuchsin, schematic diagram of (b) the microscope's internals, and (c) employed illumination optics. The microscope casing in (b) is bisected, where different physical parts are marked with different colours for visual aid, and the front half of the turret is transparent to present its details.

sec.p: The portable microscope employs a micrometric Z stage for precise focusing, as shown in Figure 1a. The stage has a maximum travel distance of 3 mm and can move the sample in the vertical direction in 2.77 mm/degree steps. A standard XY stage attached to a sample holder was employed, as seen in Figure 1a.

A 3.7 V 1800 mAh rechargeable lithium–polymer (LiPo) battery provided electrical power. The lighting apparatus in Figure 1c was attached through a USB Type A port. An electronic circuit, whose light adjustment knob is visible in Figure 1a, allows for the adjustment of the illumination level. A micro‐USB port also allows for battery recharging.

The smartphone camera adapter is a novel part of the portable field microscope, as seen in Figure 1a. It was designed as a rectangular plate with a grid of embedded magnets to ensure that any phone can be attached easily. Its design facilitates rapid alignment of the smartphone camera lens with the microscope's eyepiece.

The design of the sample illumination optics to obtain high‐resolution images deserves special attention. Figure 1c shows a schematic view of the employed lighting system. A 1 W daylight‐balanced (6500 K) power LED was used as the light source. Since the source‐sample distance is small, incident light was focused on the sample using two Thorlabs ACl12708U ½‐inch aspheric condenser lenses with f = 8 mm focal distance and high (0.78) numerical aperture, Figure 1c. The LED was placed on the back focal plane of the first aspheric lens. Between the two lenses is a Thorlabs DG05–600 ½‐inch 600 grit diffuser so that the image of the LED is not formed in the image plane of the microscope. White light was focused on the back focal plane of the second aspheric lens in Figure 1c with a spot size of around 1 mm. All illumination elements were housed in a cylindrical casing fabricated through a three‐dimensional (3D) SLA printer (Anycubic Photon Mono X‐6K). They were separated by 3D‐printed annular spacers in Figure 1c.

Prior to oocyst detection through the portable microscope, a thorough image calibration procedure was applied to ensure high‐quality and high‐resolution images for diagnosis. For this purpose, a calibration slide decorated with equally spaced bars whose width and separation are 10 and 100 µm, respectively, was employed. The calibration slide bars spanned 10 mm, where only a specific portion, depending on magnification, was visible. Seven consecutive bars were considered for analysis for each of the three available magnifications, where images were recorded to assess the portable microscope's imaging capability and maximum resolution.

The calibration slide images (Figure 3) were analyzed through MATLAB software to obtain the resolution and field of view (FoV) for each magnification in the special case of the employed smartphone (Huawei Mate 10 Pro). The specific smartphone has a 12 megapixel (MP) camera, f/1.6 aperture size and a 1/2.9″ sensor with 1.25 µm pixel size. In resolution and FoV calculations, grayscale values (GSVs) on the horizontal bisector of each calibration slide image obtained at different magnifications were calculated by first converting the colour image to a grayscale image and associating a value of 1.0 to black (whose 8‐bit colour code corresponds to a value of 0) and 0.0 to white (8‐bit code corresponding to 255).

The images of Cryptosporidium oocysts obtained through the portable microscope were also analysed using MATLAB image processing tools. The procedure is as follows: First, 120‐by‐120‐pixel images of the oocysts (such as those in Figure 5a,b) were extracted from the whole image. The second step is converting each image to grayscale. Then, the grayscale images were sharpened by the “imsharpen()” function available in MATLAB. Finally, Sobel edge detection algorithm was employed through the “edge ()” function to determine the edges of the oocysts.

sec.title: Mobile Software
sec.p: Software for both Android and iOS operating systems dedicated to the portable microscopy system was developed. It has two components, one for the registered user and the other for the certified analyst (initially, one of the authors specialized in internal medicine). Thanks to the software installed on the practitioner's smartphone, the portable microscope can be used for remote diagnosis by uploading the obtained images to be analysed quickly. For data integrity and security, the mobile software ensures that the authorized operator obtains the photos using a registered smartphone with a genuine microscope. The analysed images are stored in a database for future referral and statistical purposes. The software has offline functionality such as data visualization and images related to previous analyses. Moreover, if data connectivity is lost, the software can automatically upload pending analyses or retrieve analysed results as soon as connectivity is restored.

Figure 2 demonstrates the software's operation on the user's side. In the first step, login is required. It involves two‐step verification with a password and a short message (SMS) passcode. The second step in Figure 2 involves animal information. The animal information is automatically retrieved from the governmental Ear Tag Query System if available. Otherwise, the user must manually enter the ear tag, category, breed, sex, date of birth, and location information.

caption.p: Screenshots indicating basic analysis steps of the user‐side mobile software operating on an Android smartphone.

sec.p: The third step in Figure 2 is related to selecting the proper procedure. Although procedures for several infections, such as giardiosis and coccidiosis, are defined in the software, only cryptosporidiosis was investigated in the present case. At this step, video instructions are provided to the user so that the procedure can be applied correctly. The next step instructs the user to switch to the 100x objective and to secure the stained sample in the sample holder of the portable microscope (Figure 2). Between the third and fourth steps, the user must collect the sample on a slide and apply the staining and drying procedure properly. Checkboxes in the fourth step in Figure 2 ensure that the required steps have been followed.

The fifth step in Figure 2 involves imaging. The software asks for at least three and at most nine images obtained through the registered smartphone camera. Images can be added or deleted until submission at this step. When ready, the user submits the photos. Finally, a summary screen (Figure 2) appears, providing the user with the analysis tracking code. The user can return home and carry out more analyses at this stage. Otherwise, the software waits for a response from the analyst. When received, a push notification indicates that the analysis is complete. The whole procedure on the user side can be completed in approximately 15 min.

On the analyst side, the specialist is notified via SMS and e‐mail about submitting new images. The analyst investigates the photos and provides feedback regarding the suspected infectious agent to the user through the integrated software system. Moreover, more information regarding image quality, etc., can be provided, and re‐imaging can be requested from the user. Thus, the software platform provides an interactive means of seamless communication between the user and the analyst. This is crucial for rapid on‐site diagnosis. The whole system is patented, incorporating both hardware components and the mobile software system.

sec.title: Image Calibration
sec.p: Figure 3 presents images of the calibration slide viewed through 10x (Figure 3a), 40x (Figure 3b), and 100x (Figure 3c) objectives. Bar thickness and separation are indicated in the figures. The inset in Figure 3c shows a single bar obtained from another image for averaging purposes.

caption.p: Portable microscope images of a calibration slide as viewed via (a) 10x, (b) 40x, and (c) 100x objective. The horizontal arrows indicate the bar thickness and inter‐bar separation.

sec.p: Figure 4 shows the distribution of the GSVs on the horizontal bisector of each image in Figure 3. Figure 4 clearly shows how the same number of pixels corresponds to finer details as the total magnification increases from 100x to 1000x. The spikes around the centre in (Figure 4b,c) are possibly due to dust particles on the calibration slide. Bar‐to‐bar separation for each magnification (Except for Figure 4d) was calculated by counting the average number of pixels between adjacent bars. The bar thickness was calculated by counting the pixels between the two points at which the GSV drops below 0.5 around a particular peak in Figure 4. Then, using the data provided by the calibration slide manufacturer, the desired values were obtained and are listed in Table 1.

caption.p: The normalized grayscale values (GSVs) calculated on the horizontal bisectors of the images in Figure 3 in case of (a) 10x, (b) 40x, (c) 100x objective, and (d) the inset in Figure 3(c) obtained through the 100x objective.
The calculated number of pixels per µm and field of view values for the portable microscope as viewed through the camera of a Huawei Mate 10 Pro smartphone.

sec.p: Table 1 shows that the FoV, that is the diameter of the maximum observable disc in the object plane, decreases as the magnification increases. The calculated values are 1410, 351 and 145 µm respectively for 10x, 40x, and 100x objectives. These values agree with the FoV values of common commercial light microscopes. In contrast, a single pixel in an image obtained by the employed setup corresponds to 0.69 µm (i.e., 1.45 pixels/µm) for 10x objective, while it corresponds to 0.19 µm (i.e., 5.36 pixels/µm) and 0.07 µm (14.25 pixels/µm) for 40x and 100x respectively, Table 1. It should be kept in mind that these values do not correspond to the ultimate achievable resolution values through the optical system. The diffraction limit determines the attainable resolution.

sec.title: Identification of Cryptosporidium Oocysts
sec.p: Figure 5a,b show the images obtained from two of the faecal samples through the portable microscope. The scale bars in Figure 5a,b correspond to 10 µm. The images were obtained through 100x objective with immersion oil. Three Cryptosporidium oocysts from each sample are clearly visible as bright oval spots in Figure 5a,b.

caption.p: Microscope images of two samples obtained through (a), (b) the portable microscope, (c), (d) Leica DMi‐8 inverted microscope, and (e), (f) a Nikon Eclipse E100 upright microscope. The upper ((a), (c), and (e)) and lower ((b), (d), and (f)) rows correspond to sample‐1 and sample‐2, respectively.

sec.p: The imaging capacity of the employed system was evaluated by contrasting the obtained images with those obtained from a Leica DMi‐8 inverted microscope (Figure 5c,d) and a commonly available Nikon Eclipse E100 upright microscope (Figure 5e,f). Images through the Nikon microscope were obtained via the same smartphone camera attached to one of the device's eyepieces.

As the observed fields were not identical in the three microscopes, different oocysts are visible in Figure 5. In obtaining the images in (Figure 5c,d) a Leica 5‐megapixel CCD camera was used. Oocysts are visible in these images, too. Their sizes are comparable to those of the oocysts as viewed through the portable microscope. In contrast, apparent magnification in the Nikon Eclipse E100 microscope, (Figure 5e,f) may vary due to non‐constant eyepiece–smartphone camera distance. For an eyepiece‐smartphone camera separation of 1 cm, the FoV of this microscope when the 100x objective associated with oil immersion was employed was found to be approximately 181 µm, slightly larger than the FoV offered by the portable microscope (Table 1). However, a direct comparison of the attainable magnifications is not possible. Thus, in conjunction with the data in Table 1, it can be concluded that the developed portable microscope can provide high‐resolution, high‐magnification images for diagnostic purposes.

Figure 6 shows the GSVs of the investigated image areas in Figure 5b corresponding to oocysts #1, #2, and #3 after grayscale conversion and sharpening. The actual images are presented in the insets on the top left parts of the plots. Here, a colour scale different from the grayscale is used for easy visualization.

caption.p: Sharpened grayscale images for edge detection represented in a coloured scheme for ease of visualization, as well as the detected edges (black dots) for oocyst (a) #1, (b) #2 and (c) #3 in Figure 5b. The insets are the 120‐by‐120‐pixel raw images considered for edge detection. A grayscale value of 0.0 and 1.0 represents pure black and white, respectively.

sec.p: It can be said for the first two oocysts (Figure 6a,b) that their lipid membranes are clearly discernible. The first oocyst is almost circular, while the other two are oval. The black dots in Figure 6 are the pixels belonging to the edges on each side of the membranes. The inner wall is more clearly detectable than the outer wall, as seen in Figure 6. Analysis of the images in Figure 6 reveals that the mean size of the three oocysts is 2.82 µm. As explained below, these are among the smallest oocysts discernible with the portable microscope. Besides, visualization through three different microscopes revealed that the calculated oocyst sizes in all systems are close. We derived this conclusion as the Leica microscope's imaging software provides scale bars, allowing the user to measure distances accurately, whereas the calibration slide imaging procedure mentioned in section 2.3 was also applied for the Nikon microscope. However, since the exact positioning of the oocysts on the slides was impossible, only an approximate comparison can be made. Thus, it can be concluded that the developed portable microscope can provide high‐resolution, high‐magnification images for diagnostic purposes.

Employing the above‐mentioned approach, a thorough size analysis of the oocysts in all sample images stored in the image database was carried out. The results are presented in Figure 7a, where each bar represents a diameter range of 0.25 µm. In the case of oval oocysts, diameter is defined as the larger of the two sizes calculated over two perpendicular linear segments.

caption.p: (a) Distribution of Cryptosporidium spp. oocyst diameters calculated from the images obtained through the portable microscope, (b) average oocyst density distribution in the investigated samples.

sec.p: The oocyst diameter varies between 2.70 and 8.62 µm, Figure 7a. Approximately a quarter (24.5%) of the oocysts is in the 4.75–5.00 µm diameter range. It can be said from (Figure 7a) that most of the oocysts have diameters between 4.00 and 5.50 µm. This corresponds to approximately 88% of the total population. On the other hand, oocysts larger than 5.50 µm and those smaller than 4.00 µm are also present, Figure 7a. Thus, an imaging system should offer resolution on the order of 1 µm. This is clearly the case for the presented portable microscope.

Fayer and Ungar reported that the Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts are round or oval in the 4.5–5.0 µm size range (Fayer and Ungar 1986). Similarly, Carey et al. reported similar morphology in the 4.2–5.4 µm range in their review (Carey et al. 2004). McLaughlin et al. found a distribution between 3.4 and 5.5 µm with a mean size of 4.4 µm (McLaughlin et al. 2013). Thus, the results of the present work agree with the published data.

In our faecal samples, more than 20 oocysts (++++) were detected in 83 microscopic fields, 6–20 oocysts (+++) in 134 fields, and 1–5 oocysts (++) in 33 fields. However, on average, no fields contained less than one oocyst.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: A portable, compact, and affordable light microscopy system integrated with specialized mobile software is presented for rapid point‐of‐care diagnosis of specific infectious diseases. Easily attachable to a smartphone camera, this system effectively helped identify Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts in faecal samples of 3 to 30‐day‐old calves using a simple carbol fuchsin staining method.

With its McArthur‐type folded optical path, high numerical aperture illumination, and high‐precision focusing, the microscope can resolve µm‐scale objects like Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts. Its resolving power and field of view are comparable to conventional laboratory microscopes. The whole diagnostic process — including microscope setup, software initialization, camera alignment, sample preparation, staining, imaging, and online submission — takes about 15 min, with 10 min for stain drying.

In a validation study of 250 pre‐identified positive samples, the device accurately showed Cryptosporidium oocysts (2.7–8.6 µm, avg. ∼4.8 µm), aligning with literature. The system is applicable for field diagnosis of microscopic infectious diseases (e.g., coccidiosis, giardiasis), enabling remote expert consultation via the app. The device offers high applicability in both remote rural settings with limited diagnostic infrastructure and large‐scale farming operations requiring rapid, on‐site screening for cryptosporidiosis. This innovation enhances early diagnosis, reduces calf mortality, and prevents disease spread, offering an efficient solution for veterinarians in remote areas.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Döne Sayarcan: data curation, formal analysis, investigation, methodology, resources, software, writing—original draft. Ahmet Biçer: data curation, formal analysis, investigation, methodology, resources, validation, writing—original draft. Şima Şahinduran: conceptualization, data curation, formal analysis, investigation, methodology, project administration, validation, visualization, writing—original draft. Bülent Uluğ: conceptualization, data curation, formal analysis, investigation, methodology, project administration, validation, visualization, writing—original draft. Ahmet Cicek: conceptualization, data curation, formal analysis, funding acquisition, investigation, methodology, project administration, software, supervision, validation, visualization, writing—original draft.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: In response to the authors' application to Burdur Mehmet Akif Ersoy University Experimental Animals Local Ethics Committee, it was stated on 06.06.2024 (issue number E‐93773921‐020‐401651) that ethics committee approval was not required for the study.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: Peer Review
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://www.webofscience.com/api/gateway/wos/peer‐review/10.1002/vms3.70490.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This work is supported by The Presidency of The Republic of Türkiye Presidency of Strategy and Budget within the framework of “Mission Differentiation and Specialization Program Focused on Regional Development” (Project No: 2017K12‐41003) under the project number 2017K12‐41003‐20.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: Data available on request from the authors.
